questions: For edge-crop (issue #2): Should crop be destructive (permanently remove pixels from the clip bounds) or non-destructive (store crop offsets separately so the original can be restored)? The plan assumes non-destructive with separate cropTop/cropBottom/cropLeft/cropRight fields.
For edge-crop rendering: Should the cropped area be fully hidden, or should it show a dimmed/semi-transparent preview of the cropped region while dragging (like Premiere Pro's crop effect)?

For the ghost artifact (issue #3): Can you describe when exactly it happens — is it on internal clip drag (reordering within timeline), external drag-drop (dragging a file/asset onto timeline), or both? This affects which cleanup code path to focus on.
success_criteria: Moving a clip on the preview canvas translates it without changing its width or height
Cursor movement maps 1:1 to clip movement regardless of preview zoom level
Corner resize handles change clip dimensions as expected
Edge handles crop the clip from the corresponding side (if crop feature is implemented)
No ghost/shadow DOM elements linger after any drag operation on the timeline
Rapid drag sequences and edge cases (blur, Escape, drop-outside) leave no orphaned DOM nodes
Existing overlay interactions (text editing, double-click, selection) remain functional
assumptions: Crop will be non-destructive — stored as separate fields (cropTop/Right/Bottom/Left as 0-1 fractions), not by mutating x/y/width/height
Crop will be rendered via CSS clip-path: inset() in both preview and Remotion final render
The ghost artifact is caused by DOM element leaks in useClipDrag.ts cleanup paths, not a CSS/rendering issue — will verify during audit
The coordinate-space mismatch (screen pixels applied to composition coordinates) is a real bug causing incorrect drag speed, not just a theoretical concern
Edge crop handles are for media clips only — text clips don't need crop handles
structure_warnings: Each step section should reference at least one file in backticks.
